coredata.dc:title: Repurposing of waste ammonium sulfate as S,N-doped carbon quantum dots: A sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for the determination of tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ho, Cheng-Yuan
Lee, Ting-Wei
Li, Xin-Yu
Chen, Chiaying
coredata.dc:description:
Background Currently, many types of pharmaceuticals are used to treat disease and bacterial infections. Tetracycline (TC) is a widely applied human and veterinary medicine. However, the widespread usage and lack of awareness in the management of TC lead to its unavoidable discharge into the environment. The consequences include the proliferation of resistant strains of bacteria and adverse impacts on nontarget organisms and microbial ecosystems, which means that TC is an emerging substance of concern and that it is crucial to develop determination platforms with cost-effective and fast-response features.
Methods Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received tremendous attention owing to their great performance in fluorescent probing. In the present work, ammonium sulfate-doped carbon quantum dots (S,NCQDs) are fabricated through a facile hydrothermal synthesis pathway.
Significant Findings The results illustrate that the S,NCQDs exhibit sensitive determination ability toward tetracycline via a static quenching mechanism and possess several superior features, including a rapid response, good sensitivity and stability, broad linear detection range (1–100 μM), and low LOD value (0.1203 μM), when applied in the determination of TC. Additionally, the application of S,NCQDs valorizes ammonium sulfate waste from semiconductor manufactory waste and has great potential in fluorescence sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
Static quenching
Tetracycline
Sulfate and nitrogen doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the prepared S,NCQDs
Tetracycline determination by and selectivity of S,NCQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan and the Innovative Center on Sustainable Negative-Carbon Resources from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan, and the use of ESCA, TEM, SEM, HRTEM, and XRD belonging to the Instrument Center of NCHU.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) TEM images (Inset: particle size distribution histogram) and (c-d) HRTEM images and lattice fringe (Inset: SAED patterns) of the prepared S,NCQDs.
FTIR spectra of the CQDs and S,NCQDs.
C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p core-level XPS spectra and the fitting data of (a-c) CQDs and (d-g) S,NCQDs.
Schematic diagram of S,NCQD formation by hydrothermal synthesis.

(a) UV absorption spectrum, photoluminescence excitation spectrum with a detection wavelength of 339 nm, and photoluminescence emission spectrum excited at 280 nm of the synthesized CQDs. (b) UV absorption spectrum, photoluminescence excitation spectrum with a detection wavelength of 399 nm, and photoluminescence emission spectrum excited at 314 nm of the synthesized S,NCQDs (Inset: S,NCQDs excited at 365 nm lamp). Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of (c) CQDs and (d) S,NCQDs (excitation wavelength: 260 to 500 nm and emission wavelength: 310 to 700 nm).
(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) intensity response of S,NCQDs dispersed in varying concentrations of TC (0–100 μM) (R2 = 0.9803, [TC] = 0–100 μM).

Fluorescence intensity of S,NCQDs in the presence or absence of interfering (a) molecules, (b) metal cations, and (c) anions (TC concentration = 10 μM, interferant concentration = 0.1 μM (CIP) or 50 μM (others)).

(a) Fitting of the Lineweaver‒Burk equation from the fluorescence spectra data was conducted by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of S,NCQDs dispersed in varying concentrations of TC (0–100 μM). (b-c) UV‒vis absorption spectra of S,NCQDs, TC, actual and theoretical addition absorption curve of S,NCQDs and (b) [TC] = 48 mg/L and (c) [TC] = 24 mg/L.
Performance comparison of S,NCQDs and reported fluorescence sensors in detecting tetracycline.
Recovery of TC in various real water matrices.

head.ce:title: Repurposing of waste ammonium sulfate as S,N-doped carbon quantum dots: A sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for the determination of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung City 402, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung City 402, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, many types of pharmaceuticals are used to treat disease and bacterial infections. Tetracycline (TC) is a widely applied human and veterinary medicine. However, the widespread usage and lack of awareness in the management of TC lead to its unavoidable discharge into the environment. The consequences include the proliferation of resistant strains of bacteria and adverse impacts on nontarget organisms and microbial ecosystems, which means that TC is an emerging substance of concern and that it is crucial to develop determination platforms with cost-effective and fast-response features.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received tremendous attention owing to their great performance in fluorescent probing. In the present work, ammonium sulfate-doped carbon quantum dots (S,NCQDs) are fabricated through a facile hydrothermal synthesis pathway.

The results illustrate that the S,NCQDs exhibit sensitive determination ability toward tetracycline via a static quenching mechanism and possess several superior features, including a rapid response, good sensitivity and stability, broad linear detection range (1–100 μM), and low LOD value (0.1203 μM), when applied in the determination of TC. Additionally, the application of S,NCQDs valorizes ammonium sulfate waste from semiconductor manufactory waste and has great potential in fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, pharmaceuticals, including tetracycline (TC), are extensively employed to treat human and animal diseases and infections, increasing human well-being and ensuring sufficient meat production in the poultry and livestock industries [1,2]. TC inhibits microbial protein synthesis by binding with ribosomes and causing cell death [3,4]. The widespread usage and lack of awareness in the management of TC utilization have led to its inevitable release into the environment [5,6], which results in negative effects, such as the proliferation of resistant strains of bacteria or genes (antibiotic resistance genes, AGRs) [1,7] and adverse impacts on nontarget organisms and microbial ecosystems [8,9]. This indicates that TC is a sort of emerging contaminant.

With its hydronaphthacene ring core, TC possesses properties of refractory biodegradability [10,11], high solubility in aqueous solution (231–39,500 mg/L) [2,12], and low uptake fraction in organisms (<30%). As a result, over 70% of the consumed TC is subject to being released through feces and urine in a persistent and active form [5,13,14], and the environmental distribution and transportation of TC have been reported in river water [6], groundwater [15], well [16], municipal wastewater [17], hospital wastewater [18], and swine wastewater [6,19]. Hence, the development of an on-site method for TC determination with high sensitivity, selectivity, cost-effectiveness, nontoxicity, and rapid response is imperative and highly desirable.

Although conventional techniques (e.g., liquid chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, voltammetry methods, colorimetric methods, capillary electrophoresis, and mass spectrometry) exhibit high performance in analyzing the concentration of TC [20], they often require complex pretreatment processes, expensive instruments, time-consuming processes, and off-site experiments to achieve accurate and sensitive measurements [20–24]. Since the first discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a fluorescent material in 2004 [25], fluorescence sensing with CQDs has received tremendous attention owing to their great fluorescent probing performance, displaying outstanding features, including specific ability [26], long fluorescence lifetime and stability [27], excellent optical properties [28], facile synthesis, and nontoxicity [29,30]. The photoluminescence properties of CQDs allow them to be widely applied in biosensing [31], antibiotic sensors [32], optronics materials [33], and catalysis [34].

The doping of heteroatoms has been effectively applied to control the photoluminescence properties of nanomaterials [35–37]. In terms of surface modification agents, nitrogen (N), phosphate (P), and sulfur (S) are often used as dopant elements to tune the fluorescence of CQDs [38]. The corresponding synthetic methods can be divided into several approaches: chemical ablation, electrochemical carbonization, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment. It has been reported that hydrothermal treatment is among the most cost-effective, nontoxic, and facile methods [35,39]. In addition, the emergence of environmental awareness, sustainable development, and a circular economy have promoted the development of waste management and renewable resource recycling [40,41]. For instance, the recovery of valuable materials (e.g., melanoidins and polyphenols) was carried out in the pharmacy and food production industries by the distillation process of sugarcane molasses [42], and the recovery of metals and organic products was achieved by electrometallurgical techniques [43]. Given that a large amount of ammonium sulfate (∼200 tons/year) has been recovered from the wastewater of semiconductor manufactories in Taiwan [44], it is crucial and critical to reuse and repurpose the recovered ammonium sulfate.

The present study aims to explore the repurposing of waste ammonium sulfate derived from semiconductor manufacturers to develop a facile synthesized, sensitive, rapid response, and adjustable fluorescence sensor. The results show that with the doping of heteroatoms (sulfur and nitrogen) from ammonium sulfate, the resultant sulfur- and nitrogen-doped CQDs (S,NCQDs) demonstrate exceptional photoluminescence properties. The S,NCQDs exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity with coexisting interferents in the determination of tetracycline, while the fluorescence sensing mechanism is studied in-depth.

Para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) (>99%), potassium nitrate (99%), manganese(II) chloride, and phenol (>99%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) (>95%), sodium chloride (>99.8%), quinine hemi-sulfate salt monohydrate (≥98.0%), sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, and potassium chloride (>99.5%) were purchased from Sigma‒Aldrich. 3-Hydroxytyramine hydrochloride (DA) (>99%) and uric acid (UA) (>99%) were purchased from ACROS ORGANICS. Calcium chloride, ammonium sulfate, and ferrous sulfate were purchased from J.T.BAKER. Sodium sulfate (99.9%) was purchased from TEDIA. Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (98%) was purchased from VETEC. Ciprofloxacin (CIP) (98%) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (TCI). Aqueous solutions were prepared in water purified to ≥ 18.2 MΩ·cm.

A one-step hydrothermal process was utilized to produce a highly photoluminescent and nontoxic nanocarbon material with tunable fluorescence properties and to achieve the facile doping of heteroatoms onto the surface of CQDs. Para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) (13.7 mg) was added into DI water (10 mL) to form a mixed solution, followed by placing it into an ultrasonic oscillator for 20 min. The mixed solution was then transferred and sealed into a Teflon (23 mL)-equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200℃ for 24 hrs. After the reaction, the CQDs were cooled to room temperature and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant (denoted CQDs) was collected and stored for later usage. For the doping of heteroatoms, ammonium sulfate (0.396 g) was added during the hydrothermal synthesis process, and the resultant material was denoted as sulfur- and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (S,NCQDs).

The morphologies of the prepared samples were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-1400) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) coupled with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) (JEOL JEM-2100F). High-resolution X-ray diffractometer (HRXRD) (BRUKER D8 SSS) was used to characterize the crystalline structures. 2θ angle scanning range is set between 10° to 80° with Cu-Kα radiation (λ =1.5418 Å). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS50 FTIR Spectrometer) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ULVAC-PHI, PHI 5000 VersaProbe) were introduced to obtain further structural insight into the surface functionality and elemental components of CQDs. FTIR analysis was conducted using the KBr disk method in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (EEMs) were obtained by a fluorometer (HITACHI F-7000) to evaluate the three-dimensional relation of photoluminescence (PL), excitation, and emission. The UV‒vis spectra were acquired using a UV‒vis spectrophotometer (HITACHI U-3900) in the range of 200–850 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the prepared S,NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was applied to determine the morphology and particle size distribution of the hydrothermally synthesized S,NCQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a-b, S,NCQDs appeared as well-dispersed spherical dots with a punctiform nanostructure [45]. In inset histogram image of Fig. 1a, the S,NCQDs exhibited a narrow size distribution of 1.0–5.0 nm and an average particle size of 3.15 nm in the particle size distribution histogram, consistent with the expected particle size of CQDs (<10 nm) [25,35,45,46]. As demonstrated in the HRTEM images and SAED patterns (Fig. 1c-d), the lattice spacing of approximately 0.323 nm and 0.232 nm can be indexed to (002) and (100) lattice planes of graphite carbon [47], suggesting the polycrystalline nature of the formed S,NCQDs. In Figure S1, XRD patterns presented amorphous diffraction patterns of the silica substrates (JCPDS Card No. 85–0335) [48,49]. The result is likely ascribed to the inferior resolution of XRD analysis compared to HRTEM and SAED on quantum-size samples [48–50]. It was also reported that the broad peak (2θ = 15 to 35°) centered at 2θ = 24° was attributed to surface defects and disordered carbon atoms co-existing within CQDs during the hydrothermal synthesis procedure [51].

High-resolution core-level XPS analysis was employed to further study the elemental component and surface characteristics of the as-synthesized CQDs and S,NCQDs. As shown in Figure S2a and S2b, the peaks at approximately 531, 399, and 284.8 eV for both the CQDs and S,NCQDs corresponded to their respective O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s electrons. The additional peaks at 224 eV and 169 eV observed in the full-scan spectra of S,NCQDs were attributed to the S 2 s and S 2p electrons [59,61]. Regarding the elemental components of the CQDs and S,NCQDs, the oxygen and sulfur contents increased from 9.7% to 16.5% and 0% to 1.9%, respectively (Figure S2). As shown in Fig. 3a, the C 1 s XPS spectrum of the CQDs presented four deconvolution peaks at 284.8, 286.1, 288.2, and 291.6 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon C-C/C=C (70%) of aromatic rings, C-N (23%), C-N/O-C=O (5%), and π-π* (2%) [62]. The N 1 s peak for CQDs was deconvoluted into peaks for absorbed N species (1%), pyridinic-N (55%), amine-N (36%), and pyrrolic-N (8%) at 397.4, 399.3, 400.4, and 401.5 eV, respectively, to identify the active nitrogen sites (Fig. 3b), in line with previous results observed in the C 1 s spectra that demonstrated the presence of the -NH2 functional group [63]. In the O 1 s region of the spectrum for the CQDs (Fig. 3c), two main peaks corresponding to C-OH (58%) and C=O (42%) were deconvoluted to 531.4 and 532.8 eV and ascribed to the chemical structure of the carboxyl functional group of PABA [38,58].

Compared to that of the CQDs, the deconvolution analysis of C bonding modes in the S,NCQDs showed a decrease in the C-N/O-C=O peak at 288 eV from 5% for CQDs to 2% for S,N-CQDs, and the C-OH peak at 531.4 eV in the O 1 s spectra decreased from 58% for CQDs to 42% for S,N-CQDs. This alteration was attributed to the introduction of the sulfur functional group, which can replace the C-O functional group on PABA [64]. Additionally, peak fitting of the N 1 s spectrum of S,N-CQDs further resolved the N species transformation schemes. As shown in Fig. 3e, the pyridinic-N peak at 399.3 eV increased from 55% for CQDs to 59% for S,NCQDs, indicating the anchoring of the nitrogen dopant into the aromatic ring [65,66]. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum of S,NCQDs exhibited sulfonate functional group SOx species (x = 2, 3, and 4) peaks at 168.5, 169.6, and 170.0 eV, respectively, demonstrating that the surface functionalization of S,NCQDs occurred via the sulfonate state [59,67].

As shown in Fig. 4, according to the FTIR and XPS results, N anchored into the aromatic carbon ring and replaced carbon atoms, forming a pyridinic-N-containing chemical structure. The surface of CQD was modified with sulfur and nitrogen functional groups originating from ammonium sulfate, resulting in the successful modification of the CQD surface with heteroatoms. Further effects of heteroatoms on tuning the fluorescence properties of CQDs are discussed in a later section.

To study the optical spectral properties of the synthesized CQDs and S,NCQDs, the optical spectral properties were analyzed. The UV absorption spectrum, photoluminescence excitation spectrum, and photoluminescence emission spectrum of the synthesized CQDs and S,NCQDs are shown in Fig. 5 a-b. In the absorption band of CQDs and S,NCQDs (black lines), peaks at 225 nm and 280 nm were attributed to the π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains (C = C bands) [68,69], while the peak at 330 nm was derived from the n-π* transition of C=O bonds corresponding to carboxyl groups [70,71]. In Fig. 5a, the photoluminescence excitation spectrum (red line) ranging from 260–320 nm was obtained at a detection wavelength of 339 nm, and the photoluminescence emission spectrum (blue line) ranging from 290–400 nm was acquired at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. For CQDs doped with ammonium sulfate (Fig. 5b), an excitation spectrum (red line) ranging from 250 to 370 nm was obtained with a detection wavelength of 399 nm, while the emission spectrum (blue line) ranging from 300 to 475 nm was acquired at an excitation wavelength of 314 nm. Furthermore, the inset image shown in Fig. 5b demonstrates the strong blue fluorescence of S,NCQDs under a UV lamp at 365 nm, indicating the substantial photoluminescence features of S,NCQDs [72].

The effect of the altered surface chemical structure of CQDs by ammonia sulfate doping on the photoluminescence was further explored by recording the excitation and emission matrix spectra (EEMs) of CQDs and S,NCQDs (Fig. 5c-d). The EEMs of CQDs and S,NCQDs were collected with excitation and emission wavelengths of 260–500 nm and 310–700 nm, respectively. The contour images of CQDs and S,NCQDs showed strong fluorescence signals. The peak excitation/emission (Ex/Em) wavelength of CQDs was centered at 280/339 nm (Ex/Em). S,NCQDs was also centered at 280/339 nm (Ex/Em), with one medium strong photoluminescence at 314/399 nm (Ex/Em) and another weak photoluminescence at 360/460 nm (Ex/Em) observed for S,NCQDs. The appearance of the new 314/399 nm (Ex/Em) and 360/460 nm (Ex/Em) photoluminescence and the decreasing intensity of the 280/339 nm (Ex/Em) in S,NCQDs were ascribed to the doping effect of sulfur and nitrogen species. It was reported that the newly formed functional groups induced by dopants cause the formation of defective sites [36,38], resulting in changes in the electronic states and fluorescence profile extension to the UVA and visible region, which can facilitate a broad range of applications in fluorescence sensing to determine target substances [28,72,73].

The doping of nitrogen introduced additional energy levels between the π and π* of C=C, and the doping of heteroatoms increased the size and negative charge of CQDs and lowered the nonradiative recombination of the CQDs [36,38,66], resulting in a decrease in the band gap energy (decrease in the energy level of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO)) that causes in the redshifting of the fluorescence. Although the photoluminescence intensity of the original 280/339 nm (Ex/Em) was lower for S,NCQDs, the newly appearing peak at 314/399 nm (Ex/Em) was located near the UVA-visible light region, indicating practical applicability in fluorescence sensing and bioimaging [28,35]. The decrease in photoluminescence intensity can be ascribed to lower electron cloud density or broader size distribution of CQDs after doping [74–77]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of S,NCQDs was calculated to be 30.36% using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the standard fluorescence reference with QY value of 54.6% (Text S1). The QY of S,NCQDs was superior to previous reports [77,78]. Collectively, the incorporation of S and N from ammonium sulfate into CQDs offers new possibilities for designing CQDs with redshifted and superior photoluminescence, which was selected for the study of the determination of TC in aqueous environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tetracycline determination by and selectivity of S,NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: These S,NCQDs with superior fluorescence properties were then evaluated for their ability to quantitatively determine tetracycline at an excitation wavelength of 314 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence spectra of S,NCQDs were recorded in the presence of TC at varying concentrations, demonstrating the negative correlation of the fluorescence response with TC concentration. The results indicated that the fluorescence of S,NCQDs was quenched by the presence of TC. The quantitative quenching effect of TC was further revealed by the obtained calibration plot of the relation between F0/F and [TC] (μM) (Fig. 6b), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of S,NCQDs in the absence or presence of TC and [TC] represents the concentration of TC. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, a good linear correlation was found (R2=0.9803). The determination of relevant limit of detection (LOD) was calculated through Eq. (1):

LOD
=
3 δ
s
(1) where δ is the standard deviation of the intercept (δ =0.00087) and m is the slope of the calibration curve [79,80]. According to Fig. 6b, the slope of the linear calibration curve was estimated to be 0.0217, thereby an LOD of 0.1203 μM was established, which well indicates the practical applicability of S,NCQDs for the determination of TC in aqueous environments [1,2,13,81,82]. The obtained LOD value of S,NCQDs was comparable to or lower than previously reported values for sensing TC (Table 1 ), highlighting the superior determination capability of the proposed QDs.

Selectivity is crucial for assessing the sensing performance. The fluorescence quenching selectivity of S,NCQDs in TC determination was further examined in the presence of various interfering ions and molecules that are commonly found or potentially coexist in aquatic systems, including cations, anions, dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), phenol, and ciprofloxacin (CIP). As shown in Fig. 7 a-c, after adding ions and compounds with concentrations up to 5 times higher than that of TC, the F/F0 values showed no significant differences compared to that of the control sample (TC alone). Collectively, the good sensitivity and selectivity indicated that S,NCQDs hold great promise for on-site TC determination analysis.

Quenching of the fluorescence intensity of QDs in the presence of analytes was utilized to detect analytes. The quenching phenomenon was induced by several quenching processes between QDs and analytes, including static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), Dexter energy transfer (DET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and the inner filter effect (IFE) [87]. The Lineweaver‒Burk correlation (Eq. (2)) is generally employed to describe the static quenching process [88–92]:
(2)
(
F 0
− F
)
− 1
=
(
F 0
)
− 1
+
K
LB
− 1
(
F 0
)
− 1
[ TC ]
− 1
where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities at 399 nm without and with the presence of TC, respectively, KLB is the bonding constant related to the affinity of CQDs with the quencher (i.e., TC), and [TC] is the concentration of TC. As shown in Fig. 8a, the plot of (F0-F)−1 versus [TC]−1 depicted a clear linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9932, suggesting that static quenching was the major process responsible for the interaction between S,NCQDs and TC [88–90]. The obtained KLB was 1.31 × 104 M−1, and the quenching rate constant Kq was computed based on the following equation (Eq. (3)) [91]:
(3)
K q
=
K LB
τ 0
where τ0 denotes the emission lifetime of S,NCQDs. Given that the general lifetime of QDs ranges approximately from one to a few tens of nanoseconds (10−9 s) [87], the resultant Kq was thus higher than those typically involved in the dynamic quenching process (2 × 1010 M−1 s−1) [91,93–95], indicating that static quenching dominated the fluorescence quenching of S,N-CQDs upon TC addition.

To further explore the quenching mechanism, whether the S,NCQDs (excitor) and TC (quencher) formed fluorescent stable complexes that absorbed the excitation light but remained in the ground state was determined. As illustrated in Fig. 8b and 8c, the theoretical addition absorption curve (green line) of S,NCQDs at both TC concentrations did not correspond to the actual addition absorption curve (red line). The results demonstrated the formation of a new complex [62,87,88,92] and further confirmed that the quenching of the fluorescence of S,NCQDs by TC mainly occurred through a static quenching mechanism.

The analysis of TC in various actual water matrices was verified by applying S,NCQDs as a fluorescence probe. The recovery of 10 μM TC was determined in different water matrices. As illustrated in Table 2 , the recovery rates of TC in groundwater and tap water were 102% and 104%, respectively, indicating the feasibility of applying S,NCQDs as a fluorescence sensing platform for the determination of TC in real water matrices.

In this study, S,N-doped CQDs derived from waste ammonium sulfate have a particle size distribution of 1–5 nm with an average particle diameter of 3.15 nm and exhibit a strong blue fluorescence generated near the UVA–visible light region. The developed S,N-doped CQDs demonstrate features including facile fabrication, rapid response, high optical stability, wide linear range, and low detection limit for the determination of tetracycline through a static quenching mechanism. Furthermore, as a recovered product from semiconductor industry wastewater, the novel use of ammonium sulfate as a doping agent for CQDs realizes both waste valorization and fluorescence performance enhancement. Collectively, the compiled results suggest that the proposed S,N-doped CQDs show application potential in the on-site monitoring of tetracycline in aquatic systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan and the Innovative Center on Sustainable Negative-Carbon Resources from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan, and the use of ESCA, TEM, SEM, HRTEM, and XRD belonging to the Instrument Center of NCHU.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2023.105128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-term impact of a tetracycline concentration gradient on the bacterial resistance in anaerobic-aerobic sequential bioreactors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Daghrir R., Drogui P. Tetracycline antibiotics in the environment: a review. Environ Chem Lett 2013;11(3):209–27.
Chellat M.F., Raguž L., Riedl R. Targeting antibiotic resistance. Angew Chem-Int Edit 2016;55(23):6600–26.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic resistance in the environment: a link to the clinic?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wright G.D. Antibiotic resistance in the environment: a link to the clinic? Curr Opin Microbiol 2010;13(5):589–94.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drugs for bad bugs: confronting the challenges of antibacterial discovery

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Industrial food animal production, antimicrobial resistance, and human health

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fate of tetracycline at high concentrations in enriched mixed culture system: biodegradation and behavior
